title: Complex-table alternatives

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Complex-table alternatives; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Complex-table alternatives
sources.claim: Complex tables should be simplified or split when possible; otherwise explicit header associations and linear reading checks must preserve meaning.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Complex tables should be simplified or split when possible; otherwise explicit header associations and linear reading checks must preserve meaning.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A two-tier financial header uses unique ids and headers attributes only after a simpler table design is found insufficient. Your goal is to review a complex table and propose an accessible structural alternative. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Heading hierarchy; Captions and context. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Complex tables should be simplified or split when possible; otherwise explicit header associations and linear reading checks must preserve meaning. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Merged cells, nested headers, scrolling, and responsive transformations can destroy relationships even when the desktop view looks clear. This draft therefore rejects: More merged header cells always make a table easier to understand.

includes: Recognize when multiple simpler tables, summaries or downloadable data improve understanding.
Review a complex table and propose an accessible structural alternative.
excludes: Merged cells, nested headers, scrolling, and responsive transformations can destroy relationships even when the desktop view looks clear.

outcomes: Review a complex table and propose an accessible structural alternative.

examples.title: Complex-table alternatives: concrete case
examples.situation: A two-tier financial header uses unique ids and headers attributes only after a simpler table design is found insufficient.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A two-tier financial header uses unique ids and headers attributes only after a simpler table design is found insufficient.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Prefer a simpler table, and when complexity is necessary, define and test explicit header associations and reading order.
examples.result: The result follows because Prefer a simpler table, and when complexity is necessary, define and test explicit header associations and reading order.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: More merged header cells always make a table easier to understand.
